coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modified BiOCl for highly efficient degradation of contaminants benefited from effective generation of ·O2 −
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Cai
Liu, Huanhuan
Zhong, Junbo
Li, Jianzhang
Huang, Shengtian
Fan, Huajun
coredata.dc:description: Surface modification of photocatalyst is an effective way to improve the photocatalytic performance and can accelerate the separation of photoactivated carriers. In this paper, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/BiOCl composites were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method, which further improves the catalytic performance of BiOCl. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and cryogenic electron spin resonance (ESR) confirm the successful construction of CQDs/BiOCl. Surface photovoltage spectroscopy (SPS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and electrochemical characterization show that the presence of CQDs significantly affects the surface state of BiOCl, accelerates the separation of photoinduced carriers, and results in the enhancement of ·O2 − in the reaction system. Higher level of ·O2 − in the reaction system shows stronger photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BiOCl was studied by destruction of phenol, rhodamine B (RhB) and tetracycline (TC). The photo-destruction activity of BiOCl toward phenol, RhB and TC is significantly improved by surface modification of BiOCl by CQDs. Interestingly, when the content of CQDs is 15 mL, the degradation capacity of CQDs/BiOCl toward phenol, RhB and TC is 24 times, 4.26 times and 2.4 times of that of BiOCl, respectively. Combined with the observation results, the photocatalytic enhancement mechanism of CQDs/BiOCl was elaborated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface modification
BiOCl
CQDs
·O2 −
Photocatalytic activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the samples and photocatalytic assessment
Photocatalytic performances of the CQDs/BiOCl composites
Transfer mechanism of the photoactivated carriers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM of BiOCl (A, B) and 15 mL (C, D), TEM and HRTEM of 15 mL (E) and (F), elemental mapping of the 15 mL sample (G–J).
Raman spectra of BiOCl and 15 mL sample.
Survey XPS spectrum of BiOCl, 5 mL and 15 mL (A), Bi4f (B), O1s (C), high resolution O1s on the surface of photocatalysts (D–F).
UV–Vis DRS of CQDs/BiOCl samples, the inset is the bandgap of BiOCl (A); SPS signals of BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl samples (B).
Photocurrent responses (A); IS spectra of BiOCl and the CQDs/BiOCl samples (B).
Low temperature ESR signals of the samples.
DMPO-·OH and DMPO-·O2 − signals of BiOCl (A) and the 15 mL sample (B) under Xe lamp irradiation for 2 min.

Effect of scavenger on the degradation of phenol over the 15 mL of photocatalyst (reaction time = 40 min, scavenger dosage = 0.005 mmol/L) (A); NBT Absorbance in the different reaction systems (B) (photoperiod = 15min, NBT dosage = 0.05 mmol/L).

Photocatalytic decay of RhB (A); RhB degradation kinetic curves over all the samples (B); corresponding rate constant of RhB on all the samples (C); Spectra evolution of RhB on the 15 mL photocatalyst (D).

Photocatalytic decay of TC (A); Degradation kinetic curve of TC on all the samples (B); Corresponding rate constants of TC on all the samples (C); The spectra evolution of TC over the 15 mL sample (D).
Photocatalytic decay of phenol (A); phenol degradation kinetic curves on all the samples (B); corresponding rate constant for phenol on all the samples (C).
Cycle experiment of the 15 mL sample (A); XRD of the 15 mL sample before and after photocatalytic reaction(B).
Photo-induced charge pairs transfer between the excited BiOCl and CQDs.
Curve-fitting results of the high-resolution XPS spectra for the O 1s region (ri (%) is the ratio Bi/ΣBi (Bi represents the area of each peak).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modified BiOCl for highly efficient degradation of contaminants benefited from effective generation of ·O2 −

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong, 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong, 643000, P R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong, 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong, 643000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong, 643000, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface modification of photocatalyst is an effective way to improve the photocatalytic performance and can accelerate the separation of photoactivated carriers. In this paper, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/BiOCl composites were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method, which further improves the catalytic performance of BiOCl. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and cryogenic electron spin resonance (ESR) confirm the successful construction of CQDs/BiOCl. Surface photovoltage spectroscopy (SPS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and electrochemical characterization show that the presence of CQDs significantly affects the surface state of BiOCl, accelerates the separation of photoinduced carriers, and results in the enhancement of ·O2 − in the reaction system. Higher level of ·O2 − in the reaction system shows stronger photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BiOCl was studied by destruction of phenol, rhodamine B (RhB) and tetracycline (TC). The photo-destruction activity of BiOCl toward phenol, RhB and TC is significantly improved by surface modification of BiOCl by CQDs. Interestingly, when the content of CQDs is 15 mL, the degradation capacity of CQDs/BiOCl toward phenol, RhB and TC is 24 times, 4.26 times and 2.4 times of that of BiOCl, respectively. Combined with the observation results, the photocatalytic enhancement mechanism of CQDs/BiOCl was elaborated.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous development of industrialization, the problems of environmental pollution have been paid more and more attention [1–5]. Photocatalysis is an up-and-coming and useful tactic for purifying water contaminated by multitudinous environmental pollutants [6]. So far, to successfully fulfill of photocatalytic destruction reaction, massive semiconductor materials have been developed, such as ZnO [7], TiO2 [8], g-C3N4 [9], Bi2WO6 [10], CdS [11], Ag2O [12] and so on [13–17]. However, rapid integration of photoexcited electron-hole seriously restricts the further application of these photocatalysts [18–20]. Therefore, tremendous efforts have been devoted to breaking bottle-neck to realize the intense application of photocatalysts [21–24].

BiOCl is a p-type photocatalyst with a layered structure formed by [Bi2O2]2+ and chloride ion, its internal self-built electrostatic field can memorably accelerate the partition of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs, promoting the catalytic capability [25–30]. Therefore, BiOCl is considered as an up-and-coming and efficient photocatalyst for the treatment of organic pollutants, some studies have shown that BiOCl displays better catalytic activity than TiO2 in the treatment of perhalocarboxylate acids [31–33]. Whereas, from a practical application perspective, the photocatalytic activity of BiOCl demands to be further perfected. Among the methods applied to boost the catalytic activity of BiOCl, the surface renovation has been verified to be an available and promising way [34,35].

Carbon materials have been proved to be a good candidate for tuning the partition and transfer of photogenerated carriers [36–38]. CQDs have many remarkable merits [39], for example, good water-solubility, strong chemical inertia [40], good biocompatibility, low poisonousness and so on. CQDs have broad application prospects and have received much attention in recent years. CQDs have a conjugated π structure, they can be used as photo-excited electron transport and acceptors, and have broad application prospects in the field of photocatalysis. Based on the outstanding virtues of CQDs, if CQDs are employed to couple with BiOCl, the catalytic property of BiOCl will be affected.

Herein, to enhance the catalytic activity of BiOCl, BiOCl was embellished by CQDs. Under simulated sunlight shine, the catalytic properties of the composite photocatalysts were assessed utilizing RhB, TC and phenol as simulated contaminates. The catalytic activities of CQDs/BiOCl photocatalysts are significantly enhanced compared with BiOCl. The improved photo-destruction performance of CQDs/BiOCl catalysts profits from the efficient partition of photoactivated carriers and the expedited production of ·O2 − by two channels.
All chemicals are analytical reagents and were directly used.

To prepare CQDs, 6 g glucose was dissolved in deionized water (54 mL) and agitated for 30 min at room temperature. The obtained solution was poured into Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and reacted for 3 h (160 °C). CQDs solution was obtained after the autoclave cooling down to room temperature. To fabricate CQDs/BiOCl, 0.19 mol bismuth nitrate pentahydrate was added into 30 mL glacial acetic acid and the solution was ultrasonicated for 10 min to guarantee complete dissolution of bismuth nitrate pentahydrate, forming solution A. 0.19 mol KCl was dissolved in 30, 25, 20, 15, and 10 mL deionized water, respectively, CQDs solution with different volume (0, 5, 10, 15, 20 mL) was added into KCl aqueous solution, obtaining solution B, which was slowly added into the solution A, yielding white suspension solution. The white suspension solution was agitated for 1 h, and then was followed by hydrothermal reaction for 24 h (180 °C). Samples were collected, rinsed with deionized water and ethanol, and then was dried at 60 °C. CQDs/BiOCl photocatalysts with the different volume of CQDs were allocated as 0 mL (BiOCl), 5 mL CQDs/BiOCl (5 mL), 10 mL CQDs/BiOCl (10 mL), 15 mL CQDs/BiOCl (15 mL) and 20 mL CQDs/BiOCl (20 mL), separately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the samples and photocatalytic assessment
ce:section.ce:para: XRD, SPS, XPS, scanning electron microscope (SEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT)- •O2 −, photoelectron-chemical experiments and EPR measurements using 5, 5-dimethyl-1- pyrrolidine-N-oxide (DMPO) as capture was performed as the recipe described in our previous work [1,5].

Photocatalytic destruction of TC, phenol and RhB under mimetic sunlight was performed in a Phchem III photochemical reactor. A 500 W Xe lamp affords mimetic sunlight. The mass concentration of catalyst, TC, phenol and RhB solution (pH = 7.0) is 1 g/L, 20 mg/L, 10 mg/L and 10 mg/L, separately. 5 mL suspension solution was sampled at regular intervals and the supernatant was acquired by centrifuging. The absorbance of RhB and TC was measured at 554 nm and 358 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, separately. The concentration of phenol solution was determined by a Hitachi Primede high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The detection wavelength was 270 nm.

Fig. 2 A-Fig. 2D show the morphology of the BiOCl and 15 mL sample, and more SEM information was provided in Fig. S2. The 15 mL sample has a spherical structure. Furthermore, in contrast to the single BiOCl, the 15 mL sample shows a thinner flake structure, illustrating that the growth of BiOCl crystals is significantly influenced by CQDs, which accords well with XRD and particle size distribution (Fig.S3) results. And the D50 was illustrated in Table S1. Generally, compared with a thicker sheet of BiOCl, the thinner sheet of the CQDs/BiOCl composites provides a shorter transfer distance for carriers, manifesting high segregation of photoactivated carriers, consequently, the carriers have more opportunity to participate the photocatalysis. Fig. 2E is the TEM of the 15 mL sample; clearly, the sample holds a sheet-like structure, according well with SEM observations. Fig. 2F is the HRTEM of the 15 mL sample, the interplanar distance of 0.344 nm and 0.368 nm corresponds to the (101) and (002) planes of BiOCl, respectively. In addition, the interplanar space of 0.331 nm is attributable to the (002) crystal plane of CQDs. The elemental mapping of the 15 mL sample was presented in Fig. 2G–J, the outcomes show the presence of O, Bi and Cl. These outcomes strongly validate the successful construction of CQDs/BiOCl composite materials.

The CQDs/BiOCl samples were characterized by Raman spectroscopy. Presented in Fig. 3 , the Raman peak at 156 cm−1 and 210 cm−1 is the internal A1g and Eg vibration modes of BiOCl [2], separately. Whereas the peak intensity at 210 cm−1 decreases, moreover, for the 15 mL sample, the peak around 156 cm−1 shifts to a lower value thanks to the presence of CQDs in the composites, so it can be concluded that there is an intensive chemical interaction between CQDs and BiOCl, CQDs/BiOCl is an integrated composite rather than a simple physical combination, which is favorable for the separation of photoinduced carriers and generation of OVs.

The surface chemical environment of the samples was analyzed by XPS. Presented in Fig. 4 A, the composite samples contain Bi, O, Cl and C elements, which suggests the co-existence of CQDs and BiOCl in the samples. The results are consistent with HRTEM observation. The peaks of Bi 4f were observed in Fig. 4B, indicating that Bi3+ exists in the photocatalysts. As the content of CQDs increasing, Bi4f of CQDs/BiOCl samples moves to higher values as well as the Cl 2p in Fig. 4C. The interaction between CQDs and BiOCl induces the shift, which strongly suggests that electrons transfer happens between CQDs and BiOCl, that is, electrons transfer from BiOCl to CQDs, which is profitable to block the recombination of carriers. As demonstrated in Fig. 4D–F, the peak at 532 eV can be assigned to H2O or –OH on the surface of the sample, while the peak at 530.2 eV can be allocated to the Bi–O bond in BiOCl, and the peak at 529 eV can be designed to oxygen near the OVs [41–44,66]. OVs were detected on the single BiOCl. The content of OVs was presented in Table 2 . The results illustrate that after modification by CQDs, OVs on BiOCl are significantly increased, which agrees well with the measurements of low-temperature ESR.

For understanding the optical absorption characteristics of the samples, we analyzed the UV–Vis DRS of the samples from 200 nm to 800 nm (Fig. 5 A). Below 360 nm, the single BiOCl has a strong absorption ability. However, with the addition of CQDs, the light absorption capacity of all the CQDs/BiOCl samples boosts in the visible light range, which is due to the photosensitizer influence of CQDs and the existence of OVs in the samples. Fig. 5A shows that the indirect bandgap of BiOCl is 3.26 eV. According to the formula [45], Evb = 3.465 eV and Ecb = 0.215 eV. The potential of O2/·O2 − is −0.33 eV [46], so O2 cannot be reduced to engender ·O2 − by the electrons from CB of BiOCl owing to high potential. The separation characteristics of the photogenerated e−/h+ can be revealed by SPS technology, which is an available and exquisite technique [47]. Therefore, we can perform a detailed study of the separation of photogenic e−/h+ pairs using the SPS technique. As presented in Fig. 5B, BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl samples have different SPS response strengths in the range of 300–375 nm, and all the CQDs/BiOCl samples have a stronger SPS response than the single BiOCl. For the CQDs/BiOCl samples, the 15 mL sample has the strongest SPS response. The stronger SPS response profits from the higher isolation velocity of photoactivated e−/h+, which can improve the photocatalytic capability [48,49]. Separation velocity of photoactivated e−/h+ performs a leading role in determining the photocatalytic property, the 15 mL sample possesses the highest photoproduced e−/h+ isolation velocity, consequently, it is expected that the 15 mL sample will hold the highest catalytic performance.

For further studying the isolation properties of photoexcited e−/h+ pairs, electrochemical tests were executed. Exhibited in Fig. 6 A, the photocurrent produced by CQDs/BiOCl under the sunlight shine is higher than the single BiOCl. The measurements show that the photocurrent of CQDs/BiOCl catalyst can be promoted by CQDs. As the CQDs content increasing, the photocurrent gradually increases, accordingly. The photocurrent produced by the 15 mL sample is the strongest, but when the CQDs content is beyond 15 mL, the photocurrent intensity begins to decline. As exhibited in Fig. 6B, the impedance of the single BiOCl is larger relative to that of CQDs/BiOCl photocatalysts, and the impedance of the 15 mL catalyst is the lowest. Low impedance can quicken the isolation of carriers, which is advantageous to catalytic performance.

OVs can capture electrons to result in ·O2 − with the participation of photoactivated electrons, which decreases the reunion of e−/h+ and increases the mobility of holes [50–56]. The photoactivated electrons can be captured by OVs, solidly restraining the recombination of the photogenerated carriers because the OVs are photoinduced electron trap sites [57,58]. OVs can activate O2 adsorbed to yield ·O2 − with the assistance of the photoactivated electrons, ·O2 − can facilitate the photocatalytic destruction reaction. Therefore, BiOCl with rich OVs has high photocatalytic performance. Illustrated in Fig. 7 , BiOCl has strong ESR signals, which implies that BiOCl possesses OVs. Furthermore, compared with the single BiOCl, the OVs concentration on CQDs/BiOCl is higher than that on the pure catalyst, which suggests that CQDs/BiOCl photocatalysts will display higher ·O2 − level than BiOCl, which is consistent with the experimental results of NBT.

For identifying the active free radicals, electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements were performed. The observation results were shown in Fig. 8 . DMPO-·O2 − and DMPO-·OH signals in the BiOCl and 15 mL catalytic system were detected, which strongly proves the reaction system produces ·O2 − and ·OH. Since the photoactivated electrons of BiOCl cannot reduce O2 to produce ·O2 −, consequently, ·O2 − in BiOCl catalytic system stems from the OVs, as proven by the results of low-temperature ESR. For CQDs/BiOCl catalytic system, on one hand, partial ·O2 − originates from activation of O2 by OVs with consumption of photoexcited electrons; on the other hand, the photoproduced electrons can be transferred to CQDs, the electrons on CQDs can react with O2 adsorbed on the surface of CQDs to generate ·O2 −. Therefore, it is anticipated that CQDs/BiOCl catalytic system will yield higher concentration of ·O2 − than the single BiOCl catalytic system.

For elucidating the main role of reactive radicals, it is of absolute interest to identify the active species involved in the photocatalytic degradation process. For this purpose, trapping experiments were performed. Benzoquinone (BQ), ammonium oxalate (AO) and isopropanol (IPA) were added to the 15 mL reaction system to capture ·O2 −, h+ and ·OH, separately. Presented in Fig. 9 A, the destruction efficiency of phenol notably drops with the existence of BQ, AO and IPA in the reaction system, substantially demonstrating the presence of ·O2 −, h+ and ·OH in the photogradation system, according well with ESR characterization. The results further show that the lowest destruction efficiency of phenol was observed with the addition of BQ to the reaction system, implying that ·O2 − takes an important role in the elimination of phenol, with h+ and ·OH act as the subsidiary active species. For further comparing the yield of ·O2 − produced in the different reaction systems, NBT-·O2 − experiments were carried out. Because the reaction between NBT and ·O2 − will deplete NBT, leading to low concertation of NBT, thereby low absorbance of NBT at 259 nm implies high ·O2 − yield. The results provided in Fig. 9B show that NBT has the lowest absorbance in the 15 mL system, suggesting that the reaction system generates the highest yield of ·O2 −. High ·O2 − concentration is beneficial to accelerate the decomposition of pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performances of the CQDs/BiOCl composites
ce:section.ce:para: Catalytic capability of CQDs/BiOCl for RhB removal under mimetic sunlight shine was assessed. After 30 min in dark, all the CQDs/BiOCl composites demonstrate higher adsorption capacity toward RhB than the reference BiOCl, which is conducive to remove RhB. As revealed in Fig. 10 (A-C), with the amount of CQDs ranging from 0 to 15 mL, the destruction efficiency of RhB increases. However, when the amount of CQDs solution exceeds 15 mL, the elimination efficiency of RhB begins to decrease. The decay of RhB on the photocatalyst accords well with first-order kinetics. The photocatalytic performance of the 15 mL sample is 24 times of that of the BiOCl. As shown in Fig. 10D, with the extension of irradiation time, RhB can be effectively degraded, RhB was completely decolorization after 50 min.

For studying the universality of this photocatalyst, TC was further selected as a mimetic pollutant for degradation. Presented in Fig. 11 A, TC can be degraded rapidly under simulated sunlight. And the detoxification rate of TC over BiOCl modified by CQDs is faster than that on the reference BiOCl, and the degradation rate of TC over the 15 mL catalyst is the fastest, indicating that the 15 mL photocatalyst has the best catalytic performance. The degradation of TC accords with first-order kinetics (Fig. 11B). The rate constants of TC destruction on the sample were shown in Fig. 11C. The rate constant of TC on the 15 mL sample is 4.6 times of that on the single BiOCl. The spectrum evolution of TC was investigated, as shown in Fig. 11D, it can be found that TC can be gradually mineralized. With the gradual abatement of the absorption peak, it shows that TC can be gradually decomposed into intermediates, H2O and CO2. To sum up, all the CQDs modified BiOCl photocatalysts show higher catalytic performance than the reference BiOCl. However, excessive CQDs will inhibit the excitation of BiOCl, resulting in a drop in the activity of the sample.

To further valuate the photocatalytic treatment ability of the CQDs/BiOCl composite materials, elimination of phenol over the photocatalysts was also carried out. As shown in Fig. 12 , the adsorption of phenol on the catalysts is inappreciable, however, once exposure to simulated sunlight, phenol was rapidly decontaminated, and the elimination of phenol on the CQDs/BiOCl catalysts is faster than that on BiOCl, and the removal of phenol over the 15 mL sample is the fastest, which demonstrates that the 15 mL catalyst possesses the highest activity. Additionally, the decay of phenol on photocatalyst conforms to first-order kinetics (Fig. 12B). The removal rate constants of phenol on the sample were displayed in Fig. 12C, the catalytic performance of the 15 mL catalyst is 2.4 times of that of BiOCl. As mentioned above, CQDs/BiOCl samples show excellent photocatalytic activity under mimetic sunlight. However, high amount of CQDs is disadvantageous to the catalytic activity, excess CQDs on the surface of CQDs/BiOCl inhibits the excitation of BiOCl and drastically decreases the activity of the sample. Hua Liang et al. prepared TiO2 coated polyester fabric by DC magnetron sputtering. After 350 min irradiation by visible light, the degradation rate of RhB on the prepared catalyst reaches 70%. Zhong Shuang et al. used continuous solvent method and thermal method to prepare a new type of visible light photosensitive catalyst (Y doped BiOCl). After 60 min of visible light irradiation, the degradation rate of TC on the prepared catalyst reaches 90.6%. Dalia Sanchez-RodŕRodriguez et al. used a sol-gel method to prepare BiOCl–TiO2 composite material. After 360 min of irradiation by visible light, the degradation rate of phenol on the prepared catalyst reaches 35% [59–61]. In this experiment, the photocatalysts prepared by with the assistance of CQDs display distinct activity in destruction of RhB, TC and phenol compared with the photocatalysts reported in the existing literatures, which can be allocated to the different preparation method and light source.

For verifying the stability of the catalyst, the samples were subjected to five cycles of experiments. Presented in Fig. 13 A, the samples possess relatively high stability. The XRD profiles of the samples after the cycle experiments were shown in Fig. 13B, no miscellaneous peaks appear, which further suggests that samples have relatively high stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transfer mechanism of the photoactivated carriers
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above studies, the isolation and migration behaviors of photoactivated carriers induced by CQDs/BiOCl photocatalysts under irradiation were shown in Fig. 14 . BiOCl can be excited to yield photoactivated e−/h+ pairs under sunlight illumination. The photo-activated electrons can transfer from the valence band consisting of O2p and Cl np (n = 2, 3, 4, 5) to a conduction band filled with Bi6p orbitals, remaining h+ in the valence band. For the single BiOCl, most charge pairs will recombine; only minor photoactivated carriers take part in the catalytic reaction, displaying low performance. Whereas, after coupling CQDs with BiOCl, CQDs have strong electron transportability, which effectively improves the partition efficiency of photoactivated e−/h+ pairs [62–66]. Electrons can transfer to CQDs to manufacture ·O2 −, which is the primary active species in photocatalytic reaction. Additionally, OVs can activate O2 to generate O2 −. ·O2 − formed by two ways can quicken the detoxification of contaminants. Meanwhile, photogenerated h+ can oxide H2O and –OH to create ·OH or directly attack organic pollutants. The generated ·OH has a strong oxidizing ability, achieving high elimination for phenol, TC and RhB. In the presence of CQDs, the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/BiOCl has been significantly improved under mimetic sunlight shine.

In summary, CQDs/BiOCl photocatalysts with thinner flake structure were in-situ synthesized by a conventional hydrothermal method. Modification of BiOCl by CQDs effectively affects the morphology of BiOCl and promotes the separation of photogenerated carriers. CQDs-modified BiOCl photocatalysts have significantly enhanced photocatalytic ability toward removal of phenol, RhB and TC under xenon lamp irradiation. The degradation rate constant of phenol, RhB and TC on the 15 mL sample is 24, 4.26 and 2.4 times of that of the pure BiOCl, respectively. CQDs play a vital role in introducing of rich OVs. This work helps to understand the mechanism of CQDs modified semiconductor materials and the role of OVs in photocatalysis.

The authors claim that the submission is original and has not been previously published, is not currently submitted for review to any other journal, and will not be submitted elsewhere before a decision is made by this journal. All authors are aware of the submission and agree to its publication, no conflict of interest exists. If accepted, the article will not be published elsewhere in the same form, in any language, without the written consent of the publisher.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank for National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21777168), Science and Technology Department of Sichuan Province (No. 2019YJ0457, No. 2021YFG0278, No. 2019ZYZF0069) and Zigong City (No. 2018YYJC10), Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (CSPC201903, CSPC202105) and Key Laboratory of Catalysis and Energy Materials Chemistry of Ministry of Education & Hubei Key Laboratory of Catalysis and Materials Science (CHCL20005) funding this project.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mssp.2021.106165.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon black decorated BiOCl with largely enhanced photocatalytic activity toward removal of RhB

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Liu, C. Yang, J. Huang, R. Duan, Z. Deng, J. Zhong, J. Li, Carbon black decorated BiOCl with largely enhanced photocatalytic activity toward removal of RhB, Solid. State. Sci. 97 (2019) 105989.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effective photoinduced charge separation and photocatalytic activity of hierarchical microsphere-like C60/BiOCl

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improved photocatalytic performance of flower-like BiOBr/BiOCl heterojunctions prepared by an ionic liquid assisted one-step hydrothermal method
